module_title: Episode 2: Tea — The Way of Tea
title: Key Terms: Tea
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 2 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the Tea episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources.

## Geography

**Camellia sinensis** (kah-MEE-lee-ah sih-NEN-sis). The single plant all true tea comes from. Green, black, oolong, and white differ only by processing (Mair & Hoh, 2009).

**Theanine** (THEE-ah-neen). An amino acid found almost only in tea and one species of mushroom (Mair & Hoh, 2009).

**Climate zone.** A region with a steady pattern of temperature, rainfall, and seasons. The zone shapes tea's flavor.

**Flush.** A seasonal burst of new growth, a harvest period. The spring first flush is often the most prized (Mair & Hoh, 2009).

**Dormancy.** A cold-season pause when the plant stops growing, which concentrates flavor in the leaf.

**Terroir** (teh-RWAHR). How geography, climate, and soil show up in flavor. For tea, the climate zone does much of this.

**CTC process.** Crush, Tear, Curl, an industrial method for commodity black tea, used heavily in Kenya.

**Tea Horse Road.** An overland route where Chinese tea was carried by caravan to Tibet and traded for horses (Mair & Hoh, 2009).

**Maritime Silk Road.** The sea route by which Dutch and British ships carried tea from China to Europe from the 1600s (Rappaport, 2017).

## Social Studies

**Chanoyu** (chah-NOH-yoo). The Japanese tea ceremony, the Way of Tea, codified by Sen no Rikyu in the 1500s (Sen, 1998).

**Wabi-sabi** (WAH-bee SAH-bee). A sense of beauty in imperfection, the heart of the tea ceremony (Sen, 1998).

**Mercantilism** (MUR-kan-til-iz-um). An economic system in which colonies exist to benefit the imperial power.

**Monopoly.** Exclusive control of a commodity. The Tea Act of 1773 gave the East India Company a monopoly on colonial tea (Carp, 2010).

**Intolerable Acts.** The colonial name for Britain's punitive 1774 response, which closed Boston Harbor (Carp, 2010).

**Indentured labor.** Workers contracted under restrictive terms with little freedom to leave, used on British Assam tea plantations (Sharma, 2011).

## Economics

**Oxidation.** The processing reaction that turns a green leaf into oolong or black tea. White tea is barely oxidized (International Tea Committee, 2023).

**Value-added processing.** Transforming a raw input to raise its worth. The same leaf is worth far more as white tea than as black (International Tea Committee, 2023).

**Sequential extraction.** Many cups from one measure of leaf. A good oolong steeps 6 to 8 times, pu-erh 10 to 20 or more (International Tea Committee, 2023).

**Protected designation of origin.** Legal geographic branding. Darjeeling, like Champagne, can carry its name only if it comes from that place (Rappaport, 2017).

## English Language Arts

**Cha.** The family of tea words that spread overland on Central Asian routes, such as Hindi chai and Russian chai (Mair & Hoh, 2009).

**Te.** The family of tea words that spread by sea through the Fujian ports, such as English tea and French the (Mair & Hoh, 2009).

**Cha Jing.** Lu Yu's Classic of Tea (760 CE), the first comprehensive tea treatise in any language (Lu Yu, 760 CE).

## Sources
- Carp, B. L. (2010). Defiance of the patriots: The Boston Tea Party and the making of America. Yale University Press.
- International Tea Committee. (2023). Annual bulletin of statistics 2023. https://www.inttea.com
- Lu Yu. (760 CE). The classic of tea (trans. Carpenter, 1974). Ecco Press.
- Mair, V. H., & Hoh, E. (2009). The true history of tea. Thames and Hudson.
- Rappaport, E. (2017). A thirst for empire: How tea shaped the modern world. Princeton University Press.
- Sen, S. (1998). The Japanese way of tea: From its origins in China to Sen Rikyu. University of Hawai'i Press.
- Sharma, J. (2011). Empire's garden: Assam and the making of India. Duke University Press.

module_title: Episode 2: Tea — The Way of Tea
title: Cumulative Review: Tea
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the Tea episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. All true tea is one plant, Camellia sinensis, grown across a wide band from about 42 degrees North to 33 degrees South, and the climate zone shapes the flavor: slow mountain growth gives complex Darjeeling, hot lowland growth gives bold Assam (Mair & Hoh, 2009; Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2023). China grows nearly half the world's tea, and tea traveled both overland, on the Tea Horse Road, and by sea, on the Maritime Silk Road (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2023; Rappaport, 2017).

Now social studies. Tea went from a Chinese origin and Lu Yu's Classic of Tea in 760 CE to the Japanese ceremony Sen no Rikyu codified around wabi-sabi (Lu Yu, 760 CE; Sen, 1998). And it changed history. British tea taxes, which were a large share of government revenue, led through the Tea Act to the Boston Tea Party of 1773, where about 92,000 pounds of tea were destroyed, and then to the Coercive Acts that closed Boston Harbor (Carp, 2010; Rappaport, 2017). Hold that next to the labor: British Assam plantations ran on indentured workers, and Darjeeling workers were still striking for fair wages in 2015 (Sharma, 2011).

Then economics. The same leaf becomes teas worth a few dollars or hundreds, because tea's value comes mostly from processing, white versus black, not just origin (International Tea Committee, 2023). In commodity tea the farmer captures only about 10 to 15 percent of retail, lower than coffee, though premium tea can reach 40 to 60 percent (Rappaport, 2017; Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2023).

Last, language. Half the world says a version of "cha" and half says a version of "te," and the split is a map of how tea traveled, overland or by sea (Mair & Hoh, 2009). And tea's great texts, from Lu Yu to Okakura, treat a cup as philosophy and poetry (Lu Yu, 760 CE; Okakura, 1906).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. With coffee, geography and origin drove the story. With tea, processing drives it. One plant becomes a hundred teas because of what human hands do after the harvest, and that single fact runs through tea's flavor, its money, its history, and even its name. When you pick up a cup of tea, you are holding a plant, a process, a trade route, and a word.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. Why can one tea leaf become products worth a few dollars or hundreds of dollars per kilogram? [Beat] Because tea's value comes mostly from processing, not origin; the same leaf is worth far more as minimally processed white tea than as fully oxidized black tea (International Tea Committee, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: one plant, Camellia sinensis, across a wide range; climate zone shapes flavor; China grows nearly half (Mair & Hoh, 2009; Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2023).
- Social studies: from Lu Yu and the Japanese ceremony to the Boston Tea Party and plantation labor (Lu Yu, 760 CE; Carp, 2010; Sharma, 2011).
- Economics: value comes from processing, and the commodity farmer captures only about 10 to 15 percent of retail (International Tea Committee, 2023).
- Language: the cha and te split maps how tea traveled, overland or by sea (Mair & Hoh, 2009).

## Sources
- Carp, B. L. (2010). Defiance of the patriots: The Boston Tea Party and the making of America. Yale University Press.
- Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. (2023). Tea market report 2023. https://www.fao.org
- International Tea Committee. (2023). Annual bulletin of statistics 2023. https://www.inttea.com
- Lu Yu. (760 CE). The classic of tea (trans. Carpenter, 1974). Ecco Press.
- Mair, V. H., & Hoh, E. (2009). The true history of tea. Thames and Hudson.
- Okakura, K. (1906). The book of tea. Putnam.
- Rappaport, E. (2017). A thirst for empire: How tea shaped the modern world. Princeton University Press.
- Sen, S. (1998). The Japanese way of tea: From its origins in China to Sen Rikyu. University of Hawai'i Press.
- Sharma, J. (2011). Empire's garden: Assam and the making of India. Duke University Press.

module_title: Episode 2: Tea — The Way of Tea
title: Sources and Further Reading: Tea
text_content:
Every claim in this episode is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project.

## References

Carp, B. L. (2010). Defiance of the patriots: The Boston Tea Party and the making of America. Yale University Press.

Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. (2023). Tea market report 2023. FAO Intergovernmental Group on Tea. https://www.fao.org

International Tea Committee. (2023). Annual bulletin of statistics 2023. ITC Publications. https://www.inttea.com

Lu Yu. (760 CE). The classic of tea: Origins and rituals (F. R. Carpenter, Trans., 1974). Ecco Press.

Mair, V. H., & Hoh, E. (2009). The true history of tea. Thames and Hudson.

Okakura, K. (1906). The book of tea. Putnam. (Public domain, available via Project Gutenberg)

Orwell, G. (1946, January 12). A nice cup of tea. Evening Standard.

Rappaport, E. (2017). A thirst for empire: How tea shaped the modern world. Princeton University Press.

Sen, S. (1998). The Japanese way of tea: From its origins in China to Sen Rikyu. University of Hawai'i Press.

Sharma, J. (2011). Empire's garden: Assam and the making of India. Duke University Press.

Specialty Coffee Association. (2024). The specialty coffee almanac 2024. SCA Publications. https://sca.coffee

module_title: Episode 2: Tea — The Way of Tea
title: Knowledge Check: Tea
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 2: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, the cha and te etymology and tea's road from medicine to ceremony, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"Green, black, oolong, and white tea all come from what?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"One plant, Camellia sinensis, processed in different ways"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Four different plant species"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Different parts of the coffee plant"},{"id":"q1d","text":"A blend of herbs, not a single plant"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"All true tea comes from one plant, Camellia sinensis. The color and type are decided by how the leaf is processed, not by a different species.","citation":"Lesson: Tea, and the Plant Behind Every Cup"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"How does the tea plant's growing range compare with coffee's?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Wider, from about 42 degrees North to 33 degrees South"},{"id":"q2b","text":"Narrower than coffee's belt"},{"id":"q2c","text":"Only at the equator"},{"id":"q2d","text":"Only in cold climates"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Tea grows across a wider band than coffee, roughly 42 degrees North to 33 degrees South. It is more forgiving about location than the narrow Coffee Belt.","citation":"Lesson: The Tea Plant and Its Climate Zones"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Theanine, the amino acid in tea, is notable because it is found almost only where?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"In tea and one species of mushroom"},{"id":"q3b","text":"In all caffeinated plants"},{"id":"q3c","text":"In coffee and tea equally"},{"id":"q3d","text":"In every green leaf"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Theanine is found almost nowhere else in nature, only in tea and one species of mushroom. It is not common to all caffeinated or green plants.","citation":"Lesson: The Tea Plant and Its Climate Zones"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"A tea grows slowly at high altitude and comes out complex, with a muscatel note. Which region fits?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Darjeeling, a high mountain zone"},{"id":"q4b","text":"Assam, a tropical lowland"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Coastal Kenya"},{"id":"q4d","text":"The Rize region of Turkey"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Darjeeling's high-altitude slow growth builds complex, muscatel flavor. Lowland Assam grows fast in the heat and tastes bold and malty instead.","citation":"Lesson: The Tea Plant and Its Climate Zones"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Which country grows the most tea, about 47 percent of the world's supply?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"China"},{"id":"q5b","text":"India"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Kenya"},{"id":"q5d","text":"Turkey"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"China grows about 47 percent of the world's tea and makes the full range from one plant. India is second at about 21 percent.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the First Flush"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What is a flush in tea growing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"A burst of new growth, a harvest period; the spring first flush is often most prized"},{"id":"q6b","text":"A way of washing the leaves before drying"},{"id":"q6c","text":"A type of black tea"},{"id":"q6d","text":"The fermentation step"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"A flush is a harvest period of new growth. The spring first flush is often the most prized of the year. It is not a washing step, a tea type, or fermentation.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the First Flush"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Which country drinks the most tea per person in the world?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Turkey"},{"id":"q7b","text":"China"},{"id":"q7c","text":"England"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Kenya"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Turkey leads the world in tea consumed per person. China grows the most overall, but that is a production figure, not consumption per person.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the First Flush"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"On the Tea Horse Road, what did Chinese traders receive for their tea?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"Horses, carried over the mountains to and from Tibet"},{"id":"q8b","text":"Silk"},{"id":"q8c","text":"Gold from Europe"},{"id":"q8d","text":"Spices from India"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"The Tea Horse Road traded Chinese tea for Tibetan horses by yak caravan, from the 7th into the 20th century. It was not a silk, gold, or spice exchange.","citation":"Lesson: The Roads Tea Traveled"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What changed when Dutch and British ships began carrying tea by sea in the 1600s?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Tea moved faster and in far larger volume, turning it into an everyday European drink"},{"id":"q9b","text":"Tea became rarer and more expensive than ever"},{"id":"q9c","text":"Overland caravans immediately stopped"},{"id":"q9d","text":"Tea was only used as medicine"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Sea routes moved tea faster and in much greater volume, which made it an everyday European drink and eventually a target for taxes. It did not make tea rarer or limit it to medicine.","citation":"Lesson: The Roads Tea Traveled"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"Which legend is described as an unverified origin myth for tea?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Emperor Shen Nung discovering tea around 2737 BCE"},{"id":"q10b","text":"Lu Yu inventing tea in 760 CE"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Sen no Rikyu bringing tea to China"},{"id":"q10d","text":"The Dutch discovering the tea plant"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The story of Emperor Shen Nung discovering tea around 2737 BCE is a traditional origin myth, not verified history. Lu Yu wrote about tea much later, and Sen no Rikyu worked in Japan.","citation":"Lesson: From Medicine to Ceremony"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What was Lu Yu's Cha Jing (760 CE)?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"The first comprehensive tea treatise in any language"},{"id":"q11b","text":"A British tax law"},{"id":"q11c","text":"A Japanese tea ceremony"},{"id":"q11d","text":"A trade agreement with Russia"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"The Cha Jing, the Classic of Tea, was the first comprehensive treatise on tea in any language and treated tea as philosophy. It was not a law, a ceremony, or a treaty.","citation":"Lesson: From Medicine to Ceremony"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"Sen no Rikyu codified the Japanese tea ceremony, chanoyu, around a central idea called what?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"Wabi-sabi, beauty in imperfection"},{"id":"q12b","text":"Terroir, flavor from place"},{"id":"q12c","text":"Mercantilism"},{"id":"q12d","text":"The first flush"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Sen no Rikyu's tea ceremony centered on wabi-sabi, a sense of beauty in imperfection. Terroir, mercantilism, and flush are tea ideas, but not the heart of chanoyu.","citation":"Lesson: From Medicine to Ceremony"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"At the Boston Tea Party on December 16, 1773, what happened?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"About 342 chests, roughly 92,000 pounds of tea, were destroyed"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Colonists drank free tea in protest"},{"id":"q13c","text":"The British raised the price of coffee"},{"id":"q13d","text":"A new tea plantation opened in Boston"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Protesters destroyed about 342 chests, roughly 92,000 pounds of tea, worth around 12,000 pounds sterling. It was destruction, not free drinking or a price change.","citation":"Lesson: The Boston Tea Party"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What did the Tea Act of 1773 do?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"Gave the East India Company a monopoly on colonial tea"},{"id":"q14b","text":"Banned tea in the colonies"},{"id":"q14c","text":"Ended all tea taxes"},{"id":"q14d","text":"Closed Boston Harbor"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"The Tea Act gave the East India Company a monopoly on colonial tea while keeping the tax principle. It did not ban tea or end taxes; closing Boston Harbor came later, in the Coercive Acts of 1774.","citation":"Lesson: The Boston Tea Party"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"Roughly how much of British government revenue came from tea taxes by 1750?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"About 10 to 15 percent"},{"id":"q15b","text":"About 1 percent"},{"id":"q15c","text":"About 50 percent"},{"id":"q15d","text":"None, tea was untaxed"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Tea taxes provided about 10 to 15 percent of British government revenue by 1750, which is why losing colonial tea revenue mattered so much. It was far more than 1 percent and far less than half.","citation":"Lesson: The Boston Tea Party"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"How did the British staff the Assam tea plantations they built in the 1840s?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"With indentured labor recruited from tribal communities, held by debt"},{"id":"q16b","text":"With paid local volunteers"},{"id":"q16c","text":"With machines only"},{"id":"q16d","text":"With Japanese tea masters"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Assam plantations used indentured labor recruited from tribal communities, isolated and held by debt. This is the colonial labor reality behind the refined tea culture.","citation":"Lesson: Who Grows the Tea"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What happened in the Darjeeling strikes of 2015?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"About 300,000 plantation workers demanded fair wages"},{"id":"q17b","text":"The plantations were shut down forever"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Britain re-took control of the region"},{"id":"q17d","text":"A new tea variety was discovered"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"About 300,000 Darjeeling plantation workers struck for fair wages in 2015, part of an ongoing labor-rights struggle. It was a wage protest, not a permanent shutdown or a change of control.","citation":"Lesson: Who Grows the Tea"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"Why can the same tea leaf become a tea worth about 200 dollars a kilogram or about 4 dollars a kilogram?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Processing: minimal-processing white tea sells far higher than fully oxidized black tea"},{"id":"q18b","text":"The leaves come from different plants"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Only the country of origin sets the price"},{"id":"q18d","text":"Black tea is always worth more than white"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Value-added processing explains the gap: the same leaf is worth about 200 dollars a kilogram as white tea and about 4 dollars a kilogram as black. It is one plant, and processing, not origin alone, drives the price.","citation":"Lesson: One Leaf, Many Teas"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"Which tea is aged and fermented, with prices ranging from about 20 dollars to over 1,000 dollars a kilogram?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Pu-erh"},{"id":"q19b","text":"Green tea"},{"id":"q19c","text":"White tea"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Kenyan CTC black tea"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Pu-erh is aged and fermented and can improve over decades, with prices from about 20 dollars to over 1,000 dollars a kilogram. Green, white, and commodity black teas do not age that way.","citation":"Lesson: One Leaf, Many Teas"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"In the commodity tea system, about what share of the retail price reaches the farmer?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"About 10 to 15 percent, lower than coffee's roughly 30 percent"},{"id":"q20b","text":"About 70 percent"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Exactly the same as coffee"},{"id":"q20d","text":"About 90 percent"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Commodity tea farmers capture only about 10 to 15 percent of retail, lower than coffee's roughly 30 percent. In premium tea, the farmer's share can rise to about 40 to 60 percent.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid, Tea and Coffee Compared"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What does sequential extraction mean for tea's value?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"One purchase yields many cups; a good oolong steeps 6 to 8 times and pu-erh 10 to 20 or more"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Tea can only be brewed once"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Tea must be blended with coffee"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Tea loses all flavor after the first cup"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Sequential extraction means one measure of leaf makes many cups, with oolong steeping 6 to 8 times and pu-erh 10 to 20 or more. That stretches the value of a single purchase.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid, Tea and Coffee Compared"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What is the headline difference between tea and coffee economics?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Tea's value is driven mostly by processing; coffee's by origin and terroir"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Both are driven only by origin"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Tea has no farmers"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Coffee cannot be graded"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Tea's value comes mostly from how the leaf is processed, while coffee's comes mostly from origin and terroir. That is why the same tea leaf can become teas of wildly different value.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid, Tea and Coffee Compared"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"A language that calls tea a version of 'cha' most likely received tea how?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Overland, along Central Asian routes"},{"id":"q23b","text":"By sea through Fujian ports"},{"id":"q23c","text":"Never, those languages have no tea word"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Only by air in modern times"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"A 'cha' word marks overland arrival on Central Asian routes (Mandarin cha, Hindi chai, Russian chai). A 'te' word marks sea arrival through the Fujian ports (English tea, French the).","citation":"Lesson: Cha and Te"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"English 'tea,' French 'the,' and German 'Tee' all descend from which route?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Sea trade through the Fujian ports"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Overland caravans through Central Asia"},{"id":"q24c","text":"The Tea Horse Road"},{"id":"q24d","text":"The Trans-Siberian route"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"The 'te' family of words came by sea through the Fujian ports, carried by Dutch trade. The overland routes produced the 'cha' family instead.","citation":"Lesson: Cha and Te"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"In the Cha Jing, Lu Yu describes stages of boiling water using what kind of language?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Sensory metaphor, such as fish eyes, spring sounds, and raging waves"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Exact temperatures in degrees"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Legal definitions"},{"id":"q25d","text":"Market prices"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Lu Yu describes the boil through sensory metaphor, fish eyes, then spring sounds, then raging waves, treating tea as philosophy and craft, not a recipe of numbers.","citation":"Lesson: The Classic of Tea and the Way of Tea"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Why did Okakura write The Book of Tea (1906) in English?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"To bridge Eastern and Western understanding of tea"},{"id":"q26b","text":"To sell tea in America"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Because Japanese was banned"},{"id":"q26d","text":"To write a tax manual"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Okakura wrote in English to bridge East and West, with the line that tea began as a medicine and grew into a beverage, and entered the realm of poetry. It was a cultural essay, not a sales pitch or a manual.","citation":"Lesson: The Classic of Tea and the Way of Tea"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"When reading a tea advertisement critically, which question matters most about labor?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Whose labor is invisible, such as the plantation workers and sorters"},{"id":"q27b","text":"Which celebrity appears"},{"id":"q27c","text":"The exact font used"},{"id":"q27d","text":"The price shown"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Ask whose labor is hidden, the plantation workers, sorters, and processing experts who rarely appear. Celebrity, font, and price are not the core of a media-literacy reading.","citation":"Lesson: Reading a Tea Ad"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"Tea is more particular than coffee about its soil. What does it want?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Acidic soil, even more acidic than coffee likes (about pH 4.5 to 5.5)"},{"id":"q28b","text":"Salty coastal soil"},{"id":"q28c","text":"Dry desert sand"},{"id":"q28d","text":"Alkaline soil"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Tea wants acidic soil, around pH 4.5 to 5.5, more acidic than coffee prefers. It is not a desert, salt, or alkaline crop.","citation":"Lesson: The Tea Plant and Its Climate Zones"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"India is the world's second largest tea producer. What is it most famous for?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Black tea from Assam and Darjeeling"},{"id":"q29b","text":"White tea only"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Oolong from the Rize region"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Pu-erh from Yunnan"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"India is second at about 21 percent of world tea and is famous for black tea from Assam and Darjeeling. Rize is in Turkey, and pu-erh comes from China.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the First Flush"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"The Trans-Siberian caravan route carried tea from China to where, and how long could it take?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Through Mongolia to Russia, a trip that could take about 16 months"},{"id":"q30b","text":"To Tibet in a single week"},{"id":"q30c","text":"To Boston in 18 days"},{"id":"q30d","text":"To Japan by ferry"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"The Trans-Siberian caravan carried tea by camel from China through Mongolia into Russia, a journey that could take about 16 months. The Tibet route was the Tea Horse Road, and the fast modern routes are by sea.","citation":"Lesson: The Roads Tea Traveled"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"Eisai is remembered for what, around the year 1191?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Bringing tea seeds from China to Japan, beginning Japanese tea culture"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Leading the Boston Tea Party"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Writing the first British tea tax"},{"id":"q31d","text":"Inventing the CTC machine"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Eisai brought tea seeds from China to Japan around 1191, which helped begin Japanese tea culture that Sen no Rikyu later formalized. The other events are centuries apart and elsewhere.","citation":"Lesson: From Medicine to Ceremony"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"The Coercive Acts of 1774 (the Intolerable Acts) responded to the Tea Party by doing what?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Closing Boston Harbor"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Lowering all tea taxes"},{"id":"q32c","text":"Granting the colonies independence"},{"id":"q32d","text":"Banning coffee"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"The Coercive Acts punished Boston by closing its harbor and tightening control. They did not lower taxes, grant independence, or touch coffee.","citation":"Lesson: The Boston Tea Party"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"Darjeeling tea is protected the way Champagne is. What is that protection called?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"A protected designation of origin, legal geographic branding"},{"id":"q33b","text":"A patent on the plant"},{"id":"q33c","text":"A trademark on the color"},{"id":"q33d","text":"A government subsidy"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Darjeeling has a protected designation of origin, a legal geographic brand like Champagne, so only tea from that place can use the name. It is not a plant patent, a color trademark, or a subsidy.","citation":"Lesson: Who Gets Paid, Tea and Coffee Compared"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"When did fair trade tea certification begin?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"1988"},{"id":"q34b","text":"1773"},{"id":"q34c","text":"760 CE"},{"id":"q34d","text":"2015"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Fair trade tea certification began in 1988, part of the ongoing response to plantation labor concerns. 1773 was the Boston Tea Party, 760 CE was Lu Yu's treatise, and 2015 was the Darjeeling strikes.","citation":"Lesson: Who Grows the Tea"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"About how large is the global tea market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"About 55 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q35b","text":"About 1 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q35c","text":"About 500 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Larger than the coffee market"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"The global tea market is about 55 billion dollars on roughly 6 million tonnes a year. That is smaller than coffee's market of about 245 to 269 billion dollars.","citation":"Lesson: One Leaf, Many Teas"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What gives white tea its high wholesale price compared with black tea?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Minimal processing of young leaves, which limits supply and raises value"},{"id":"q36b","text":"Heavy machine processing"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Full oxidation"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Being grown only in Kenya"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"White tea is barely processed, just picked and dried, and sells at a high wholesale price, around 120 to 200 dollars a kilogram. Black tea is fully oxidized and far cheaper, around 4 to 15 dollars a kilogram.","citation":"Lesson: One Leaf, Many Teas"}]}
